# Budget Briefing for Jess (Anxious Pre-K Parent)

**What the FY27 budget means for families entering South Portland schools**

---

## The Most Important Thing First

**Full-day kindergarten is not being cut.** Your child will have a full-day K program. The changes are about *which buildings* serve which students, not about eliminating grade levels or programs.

## Your Child Is Entering a System in Transition

South Portland schools are reorganizing -- closing one elementary school, reducing staff, and absorbing new pre-K students. That sounds alarming. Here's what it means for an incoming family.

**What's staying:** Full-day kindergarten. Four elementary schools (Brown, Kaler, Small, Skillin). The Community School model, which has cut chronic absenteeism in half at the middle school and served nearly 1,000 students through its health center. Pre-K services expanding through a state mandate.

**What's changing:** Dyer Elementary is proposed to close in September 2026. Students would be redistricted to the other four schools. The district is cutting 42 teachers and 16 ed techs across all schools. Class sizes will be larger.

**What's not decided yet:** The board hasn't voted. March 30 is the expected vote. You still have time to ask questions and participate.

## Which School Will My Child Attend?

That depends on where you live -- and the redistricting maps haven't been published yet.

**If you're not sure which school zone you're in now**, check the district website (spsdme.org) or call the central office. Knowing your current zone helps you understand how the redistricting might affect you.

If you're in the Dyer zone, your child would be assigned to a different school. If you're in one of the other four zones, your school stays open but may receive additional students from Dyer.

**Bottom line:** Watch for redistricting maps. They should be published before the board votes.

## What About Pre-K?

A state mandate is bringing **80-90 four-year-olds** with special education needs into the district starting September 2026 (three-year-olds follow in FY28). The district is also exploring a **Head Start partnership** (details expected in March).

This means the district is investing in early childhood -- the pre-K pipeline is growing, not shrinking. But it's happening at the same time as staff cuts, which creates a real tension. **Watch for the Head Start partnership announcement in March** -- that's where you'll get the clearest picture of what the pre-K program looks like when your child is eligible.

## Is This a Good School System to Enter?

The honest answer: it's a system under financial stress that is still doing some things well.

**What's working:** The Community School model at SPMS has produced measurable results -- chronic absenteeism dropped from 22% to 10.5%, parent conferences up 22%, 7,000 pounds of food distributed through the Locker Project. The board voted unanimously to expand it. The pre-K expansion is a forward-looking investment.

**What's hard:** The district has an $8.4M budget gap, no savings left, 78 positions being cut, and the state funds only 20% of actual costs (supposed to be 55%). Enrollment is down 11% over the past decade. The district has more expensive needs with fewer students to spread costs across.

**The trajectory question you're really asking:** By the time your child enters kindergarten, the district will likely have four elementary schools, expanded pre-K services, and a growing Community School program -- but with larger class sizes and fewer support staff. The biggest unknowns are class sizes and whether your school zone changes.

## What to Watch For

- **Redistricting maps** -- which neighborhoods feed into which schools after Dyer closes
- **Pre-K programming details** -- Head Start partnership, CDS transition plan
- **Class sizes at the elementary level** -- 42 fewer teachers means larger classes
- **The June 9 referendum** -- if the budget fails the public vote, the district must cut further

## How to Get Involved

If bedtime makes evening meetings hard to attend, you have options:

- **Submit questions** at spsdme.org/budget27 before the March 16 meeting -- district leadership will respond
- **Watch recordings** of meetings on the SPC-TV Vimeo channel, on your own schedule
- **March 23 (6 PM, Redbank Community Center)** is the single most important meeting -- first time detailed numbers go public. Worth arranging childcare for if you can.
- **June 9:** Vote in the budget referendum

You're not the only one trying to figure this out. Ask other parents in your playgroup or daycare what they've heard.

---

*Based on all public budget documents and meeting proceedings through March 10, 2026.*
